title: Folding Sewing Table
summary: A rolling cabinet that unfolds into a 6-foot-plus cutting table — hinged leaves on both sides, folding legs, and the whole thing from one sheet of plywood.
description:
Sewing needs a huge table occasionally and floor space the rest of the time, and this build refuses to compromise on either: a compact rolling cabinet with a hinged leaf on each side, each leaf carried by a folding 1x3 leg. Open, it's a cutting table over six feet long; folded, it's a cabinet that rolls into a corner. One sheet of 3/4" plywood does everything but the legs.

The cut list hides one instruction in capital letters — the legs say MEASURE THESE — and the build order explains why: the legs are cut to the distance from the leaves' undersides to the caster bottoms of YOUR assembled cabinet, not to the list's 29-1/4". Casters vary; the measurement doesn't lie.

The stability detail is in the final step and easy to miss: the legs should open slightly PAST 90°, splayed in opposite directions. A leg at exactly 90° can fold under sideways pressure — past-center, pressure seats it tighter instead. That one detail is the difference between a table and a trap. Fold-out leg hinges or locking braces are the belt-and-suspenders upgrade if the table will see a rotary cutter leaned on hard.

tools.note: Breaking the sheet into three 32" x 48" pieces, then the smaller cuts — a straightedge guide keeps the long rips true.
Pilot the hinge screws — 3/4" ply edges split with unpiloted screws.
The legs are CUT TO MEASURE off your assembled cabinet — the tape decides, not the list.
The whole top — fabric snags on anything the sander missed.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4" sanded, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: The entire cabinet and both leaves.

materials.name: Hinges, 2-1/2" to 3"
materials.note: Five sets: leaves to cabinet, legs to leaves.

materials.name: Self-tapping wood screws, #8 x 2"
materials.note: Trim screws work — smaller heads on visible plywood.

cutList.note: NOMINAL — measure your assembled cabinet, leaf underside to floor, and cut to that.

steps.title: Break down the sheet
steps.body:
Support the plywood on scrap 1x boards or foam, mark it every 32" across the width, set the saw just past the plywood's thickness, and cut three equal 32" x 48" pieces. Then cut each down per the list.

The good face goes DOWN for circular saw cuts — tear-out lands on the underside where nobody sews.

steps.title: Assemble the cabinet box
steps.body: Screw the cabinet together per the diagram — top, bottom, sides, and the interior shelf — with the 2" screws, pilot-drilled. The shelf is fabric-stash storage and, structurally, the box's mid-span stiffener.

steps.title: Add the casters
steps.body: Screw the four casters to the cabinet base with their pan-head screws. Locking casters are worth the dollar upgrade — a cutting table that creeps while you cut is a special kind of maddening.

steps.title: Hinge the leaves
steps.body: Lay the cabinet FACE DOWN on the floor with a leaf positioned against each side, and attach the hinges across the undersides. Working face-down like this self-aligns the top surfaces — hinged upright, you'd be shimming and cursing.

steps.title: Measure and hang the legs
steps.body:
Measure from the underside of a raised leaf to the bottom of the wheels — THAT is your leg length; cut the four 1x3s to it. Hinge each leg to the underside of its leaf.

The list's 29-1/4" is a placeholder for a cabinet with these exact casters. Yours is whatever the tape says.

steps.title: Tune the splay and test
steps.body:
Flip the table upright, raise the leaves, and swing the legs into place. They should open slightly PAST 90°, splayed in opposite directions — past-center means downward pressure seats them tighter instead of folding them. Trim leg length if a leaf sits proud.

Lean on it, roll it, fold it, unfold it. Then sand the entire top until fabric glides, and go cut something out.

images.alt: Rolling sewing cabinet with both hinged leaves raised into a long cutting table
